Yes, we do. And can you hear me? Yes, we can hear and see you. Welcome. Great. Uh, Thanks for joining us. A special hello from, uh, sunny Christchurch.

My name is Brendan Winder. I manage to civil defense emergency management team here at Crosser City Council. And thanks for the opportunity to speak to this bill. Christchurch city council supports modernizing New Zealand's emergency management law. The risk landscape has changed. We face more frequent weather, ongoing seismic risk, and more complex compounding, consecutive and cascading risks that stack up and spill over.

We need a clearer system that builds capability. Our submission focuses on improvement. We support the direction of travel. Our concern is simple and twofold. Can what parliament intends actually be delivered and how will it be funded? Emergency management comes in four parts, reduction, readiness, response and recovery, excuse me.

And day-to-day community facing work reduction and readiness matter. Most, they cost much less than response and recovery, and they build resilience before disaster strikes. But the builders that wait to these two areas, it leans heavily towards response and recovery. That misses a chance to prevent the very outcomes that that drove. This reform readiness only works when expectations are clear.

Plans are tests that in capability can be shown in practice. High level plans on the shelf do not build resilience. If the, if the system keeps valuing documents over local preparedness, we may stay stuck in response mode. We'll keep reacting instead of reducing risk before it hits. If Parliament wants reduction in readiness in the form of the in front of the system, the law and its regulations must set out miserable expectations.

They must also be clear about who pays. The bill requires national and regional plans on their own. These have limited impact locally. A framework that includes local planning would give agencies and communities a clear line of sight and make plans work in practice as drafted. The planning reforms risk a gap between formal plans and real world delivery.

If capability sits locally, accountability must also sit locally. Otherwise, when performance is tested, ownership will be blurred. The bill strengthens the director general's powers to drive national consistency, and that is appropriate. But councilors do need to see the assurance framework. If compliance powers increase, monitoring and expectations must also be transparent.

Councils need to know what will be measured, how often, and against what benchmark benchmarks. Without their clarity, investment becomes reactive and less effective. A predictable and proportional assurance regime support, steady improvement, operational guidance also needs care. Emergency management is not just about strategy, it's also practical, pragmatic and time critical.

Interoperability standards and clear doctrines shake what happens in a remote community at 2:00 AM in a storm, for example. Changing controller role names without aligning them with the emergency services creates more confusion. This defeats the purpose of the bill. National guidance must stay grounded in local operations. If new structures and roles lift expectations, standards and templates must be co-designed and aligned.

Workforce capability and capacity cannot be built overnight. The biggest fiscal risk in this bill sits in the broad rulemaking powers. Regulations can set in technical standards, training requirements, and reporting duties over time. These can grow without changes to the act. Consultation matters, but it does not control cost.

If compliance expectations rise, cost impacts, safeguards and clear funding rules must also strengthens. Councils need to know what is required by when and who will fund it. This brings us to the central tension in the bill. The bill lists expectations across governance, planning, assurance, and work face workforce capability.

At the same time, go government signals, rates, keeping and tighter fiscal limits. For councils, this creates a structural clash. Councils asked to do more with their main revenue tool was constrained. This is not abstract. Emergency management relies on people. It needs train controllers, recovery manage planners, community engagement specialists, and tested arrangements.

The bill assumes as capable exists or it can scale quickly. Nationally, that depth does not yet exist at the level implied. If consistency is the goal, co-investment must be a part of the answer without uplift will be slow and uneven. Consistency without funding creates compliance risk. And more importantly, capability gaps. We welcome the bill's, focus on communities that are disproportionately affected.

Meaningful engagement, however, it takes time and skilled people to enable councils to deepen engagement with vulnerable communities. Funding must match that expectation. Otherwise, engagement risks becoming a box ticking exercise. We support more inclusive, inclusive governance structures mandates authority and funding responsibilities must also be clear.

Complexity should not turn, turn into confusion. The legislation will shape New Zealand's emergency management system for decades. It is a significant step forward. Its success would depend less on structure and more about how it's put into practice. The core question for us is straightforward. If Parliament wants higher and more consistent capability across the country, who will fund that uplift?

Christchurch City Council supports modernization. We support stronger reduction in readiness. We support national consistency. We ask that funding assurance and accountability match the bill's ambition. Thank you. Happy to take questions.
